coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified oxygen doped carbon nitride nanosheets with enhanced hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Ou-Yang, Jie
Zhu, Kai
Li, Xue
Zhu, Yuxin
Song, Yanhua
Cui, Yanjuan
coredata.dc:description: The development of catalysts with strong broad-spectrum absorption for visible light hydrolysis to generate H2 has been becoming an important research fields for clean energy conversation. In this work, a in-situ two-step thermal polymerization method was used to introduce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into oxygen doped graphitic carbon nitride (CNO) to obtain modified products CNOC with different content of CQDs. The as prepared samples were characterized by XRD, TEM, BET, PL, Electrochemical experiments and so on and photocatalytic H2 production from water was used to evaluate the performance of the catalyst. Relative to CNO, the porosity and specific surface areas of CNOC-1.0 significantly increased; the visible light absorption range was extended, and the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers was improved. As a result, CNOC-1.0 decorated with 1.0 wt. % of CQDs shows the best photocatalytic activity. The corresponding H2 evolution rate (HER) is 316.6 μmol•h−1, which is 2.1 times of CNO nanosheets (154.5 μmol•h−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen doped carbon nitride
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
H2 production

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX20_3152 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190981 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL curve of the CQDs excited by different wavelengths of light.
SEM image (a) and TEM images of CNOC-1.0 (b-f).
XRD patterns of CNO and CNOC samples.
FT-IR patterns of CNO and CNOC samples.
UV-Vis absorbance patterns of CNO and CNOC samples (a). Kubelka-Munk spectra of samples (b).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (a) and the corresponding BJH pore-size distribution (b) of CNO and CNOC-1.0.
XPS survey spectra of CNOC-1.0 and CNO (a), high resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s (b-d).
Photocatalytic H2 production of catalysts: Cumulative amount of H2 with light irradiation time (a) and H2 production stability of CNOC-1.0 (b).
Photocatalytic H2 production of catalysts: with different deposited Pt content (a), with the illumination of different wavelength of monochromatic light (b).
Steady-state photoluminescence emission spectra (a), Time-resolved photoluminescence spectrum (b), EIS (c) and photocurrent spectra (d) of CNO and CNOC-1.0.
Mott-Schottky plots of CNO and CNOC-1.0 (a), schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the CNOC under visible light irradiation (b).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified oxygen doped carbon nitride nanosheets with enhanced hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of catalysts with strong broad-spectrum absorption for visible light hydrolysis to generate H2 has been becoming an important research fields for clean energy conversation. In this work, a in-situ two-step thermal polymerization method was used to introduce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into oxygen doped graphitic carbon nitride (CNO) to obtain modified products CNOC with different content of CQDs. The as prepared samples were characterized by XRD, TEM, BET, PL, Electrochemical experiments and so on and photocatalytic H2 production from water was used to evaluate the performance of the catalyst. Relative to CNO, the porosity and specific surface areas of CNOC-1.0 significantly increased; the visible light absorption range was extended, and the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers was improved. As a result, CNOC-1.0 decorated with 1.0 wt. % of CQDs shows the best photocatalytic activity. The corresponding H2 evolution rate (HER) is 316.6 μmol•h−1, which is 2.1 times of CNO nanosheets (154.5 μmol•h−1).

ce:section.ce:para: As a metal-free polymeric semiconducting material with good photoelectric properties and excellent chemical stability, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted amazing attention in the field of photocatalysis (H2 production from water, pollutants degradation, CO2 photoreduction, selectively oxidation, and so on) [1–6]. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of pristine g-C3N4 is far from satisfactory because of its weak absorption of visible light, limited specific surface area and extremely fast recombination of photo-generated carriers [7].

Even so the participation and modification of metal components can significantly improve the catalytic performance of g-C3N4, construction of complete nonmetallic materials still a major challenge in the field of functional materials. Carbon family, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene and CQDs, has a long history in the field of materials research and their applications are extremely wide [8–11]. As a new type of "zero-dimensional" carbon nanomaterials, CQDs have been widely applied in the field of photocatalysts construction because of their excellent conductivity, quantum effect, high aqueous solubility and chemical durability [12–14]. So many literatures have been reported for photocatalysts decorated with CQDs, such as TiO2-CQDs [15], C-dots/CuWO4 [16], CQDs/BiMoO6 [17], etc. As active ingredients, CQDs also have been successfully deposited onto the backbone of g-C3N4. The photo-sensitized effects and stimulated charge transfer properties by CQDs decoration could enhance photocatalytic activities of CQDs-based g-C3N4 composites [18,19]. Very recently, Kang et al. reported the fabrication of carbon dots modified g-C3N4 nanocomposite and demonstrated its excellent performance of near-IR (NIR) light-induced H2 evolution from water [12]. Shi et al. found that CQDs modified g-C3N4 showed impressive performance for photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B and tetracycline hydrochloride [20].

Moreover, elemental doping routes using various non-metallic elements with different electronegativity (B, S, O, etc.) have been explored to regulate the electronic structure and band gap of g-C3N4 [21]. Unlike metal ion doping, non-metallic elements are generally considered to replace C or N in g-C3N4 skeleton to form substitutional doping, and the adjoint introduced lattice defects are beneficial to improve the separation of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, the impurity elements may also introduce new energy levels below the conduction band of g-C3N4, thus reducing the forbidden band width of the whole system and expanding the visible light absorption range. Among them, oxygen is an inevitable co-existing element in the composition of g-C3N4. By additional oxygen doping, increased defects and improved charge ionization could be realized without the introduction of extra elementals [22]. Moreover, direct thermal exfoliation is a simple and effective way to fabricate ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets, which has sufficient surface contact to participate in heterogeneous catalytic reactions [23].

In this work, we aimed to combine oxygen element doping and CQDs decoration technologies on g-C3N4 modification to enhance its photocatalytic performance. CQDs were in situ decorated in oxygen doped g-C3N4 (CNO) by a two-step thermal polymerization method to obtain products CQDs@CNO (CNOC). The morphology and structure of CNOC were investigated by serious characterization techniques and photocatalytic H2 production from water was used to evaluate the properties of the photocatalysts. As a result, after modified with CQDs, the visible-light harvesting capability and photogenerated charge carriers’ separation of obtained CNOC were greatly increase and improve.
All of the materials were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method according to previous reported method [24]. Citric acid (1.05 g) and ethylenediamine (335 μL) were added into ultrapure water (10 ml). Then the above solution was autoclaved in a Teflon container and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove any particles and dried overnight at 80 °C to obtain CQDs powder.

CNOC was prepared through direct successive thermal polymerizations. Specifically, dicyandiamide (4 g), urea (4 g) and oxalic acid (3.2 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (20 mL), then certain amount of CQDs stock solution (1 g/L) was added into this solution and the mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Then oil bath evaporated at 60 °C, solid precursor was obtained and put into a crucible with a cover, heated at 550°C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 5°C/min and naturally cooling to room temperature. Next, the collected product was heated at 550°C for another 2 h without a cover to obtain final CNOC nanosheets. Different samples synthesized with CQDs stock solution (0.3, 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 ml) were labeled as CNOC-0.3, CNOC-0.5, CNOC-1.0 and CNOC-1.5, respectively.
CNO was prepared under identical condition without CQDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the photocatalysts were obtained on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm). Morphology of the samples were observed by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Nano SEM 230) and Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). Specific surface areas and pore size distributions of the products were measured with a JW-BK122W instrument and analyzed by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained using a UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, SHIMADZU), and BaSO4 was used as a reflectance standard. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer with Mg Kα radiation as the excitation source. All the binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak at 284.4 eV of the surface adventitious carbon. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded at room temperature on a QuantaMasterTM fluorescence spectrometer. Electrochemical experiments were taken on a CHI660D workstation performed in a typical three electrode cell, using a Pt piece and calomel electrode as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. 0.2mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used in electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared on a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) transparent conductive film glass.

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples was evaluated by the photocatalytic H2 evolution from water under visible irradiation. In the specific reaction, 50 mg of photocatalyst powder was dispersed in an aqueous solution (100 ml) containing 10% (v/v) of triethanolamine, and 3% (w/w) of Pt (H2PtCl6•6H2O, 0.04 g/ml, 100 μL) was added. After stirred for 30 minutes and vacuuming, a 300 W xenon lamp (400 nm cut filter) was used for illumination. The produced H2 was automatically sampled and detected online by gas chromatography (Thermal conductivity detector detector, N2 is carrier gas).

First, the successfully synthesis of CQDs was confirmed by fluorescence emission spectra [25–27]. Strong fluorescence and usually unique excitation-dependent fluorescence are the characteristic optical properties of CQDs, because of the multiple electronic transitions between multiple energy levels which is formed from the multiple functional groups on the surface of CQDs [28]. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of synthesized CQDs aqueous solution (1 g/L) were recorded under different wavelength excitation ranging from 300 to 460 nm and was shown in Fig. 1 . The obtained CQDs dispersion shows identifiable PL emission peaks at wavelength from 400 to 600 nm. When the excitation wavelength is shorter than 380 nm, the excitation is independent. As the excitation wavelength increase, excitation-dependent appears. The obtained CQDs aqueous remains clear after being stored for a week without any precipitation, indicating the well stability of quantum dots particles.

The microstructure and morphology of the CNOC samples (take CNOC-1.0 as an example) have been characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the synthesized CNOC-1.0 displays typical lamellar accumulation with amorphous particles. Compared with conventional g-C3N4 synthesized from one step calcination, post-thermal treatment could conduce “shatter” the bulk structure, therefore increase the porosity of samples. Meanwhile, randomly dispersed ultrathin “velvet” nanosheets were obtained by direct oxidation-etching, and abundant spongy porosity could be observed in Fig. 2b and c. In addition, high resolution

TEM images (Fig. 2d-f) depict the attachment of CQDs on the CNO nanosheet with the average diameters of 3.5 nm. These results indicate that CQDs are successfully decorated onto porous CNO nanosheets by in-situ polymerization and post-thermal exfoliation process.

XRD analysis was carried out to investigate the phase structures of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 3 displays the XRD diffraction peaks of CNO and CNOC samples with different CQDs contents. Consistent with previous reports, all samples possess two diffraction peaks at 12.9° and 27.3°, which are derived from the in-plane structural packing motif of tri-s-triazine units (100 facet, d=0.69 nm) and interlayer stacking of aromatic segments indexed as (002) facet (d=0.32 nm) [23]. For CNOC samples, the characteristic peaks of CNO maintained and no decrease could be observed even with the increasing loading amount of CQDs. Similar patterns as those of CNO and CNOC implies that the polymerization structures of CNO were not been affected by the integration with the CQDs. No characteristic peaks of the CQDs were discovered due to the low quantities and amorphous crystallinity. It also indicates indirectly the high dispersion and well attachment of the CQDs onto CNO nanosheets.

FT-IR spectra of CNO,CQDs and CNOC with different CQDs contents were shown in Fig. 4 . The vibrations at 1242 cm−1(C-O), 1398 cm−1(C-H), 1620 cm−1 (C=C), 1720 cm−1(C=O) corresponding to carbon skeleton vibration are observed for CQDs, indicating the successfully synthesis of CQDs. Except for CQDs, all spectra of CNOC display similar FTIR peaks to CNO. The sharp absorption band at approximately 810 cm−1 and strong absorption bands in the range of 1200-1600 cm−1 could be attributed to the breathing mode of tri-s-triazine units and stretching vibration of the C-N heterocycle, respectively. The broad bands at about 3200 cm−1 could be assigned to the terminal amino group and adsorbed hydroxyl species [29]. The main skeleton structure of CNO is not changed by the modification of small amount of CQDs.

Optical properties play vital role in determining photocatalytic performance of semiconductors. As shown in Fig. 5 , the optical absorption characteristics of CNO and CNOC-1.0 samples were performed by UV-Vis absorption spectra. The absorption band-edge of CNO appears at about 480 nm. After introduction of CQDs, the absorption edges of CNOC-1.0 slightly red-shift to 496 nm along with improved visible-light tail absorption. Especially, an outstanding uplifted absorption band could be observed at 480~600 nm. Enhanced visible-light absorption is one of the essential parameters to improve the utilization of light energy. Fig. 5b shows the estimated optical band gaps (Eg) of as-prepared samples by the Kubelka–Munk function [30]. The band-gap value of CNOC-1.0 is 2.79 eV, which shifts from 2.82 eV of CNO to a lower position. The incorporation of CQDs into semiconductor materials can promote the upward shift of the valence band edge to form a new valence band position, which shortens the Eg of the catalyst and broadens the spectral response range [11]. The narrowed band-gap and increased optical absorption are beneficial to form more photogenerated charge carriers and to improve the photocatalytic performance of the catalysts.

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution curves were used to investigate surface area and porous structure of samples. As shown in Fig. 6 a, both CNO and CNOC-1.0 display typical IV N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms with a H3-type hysteresis loop, demonstrating the existence of abundant mesopores [31]. Centralized mesoporous distribution with about 2.8 nm are further verified by Fig. 6b. The calculated BET specific surface area of CNO and CNOC-1.0 are 58 and 96 m2•g−1, respectively. What's more, enlarged pore volume of CNOC-1.0 (0.839 m3•g−1) is obtained compared to CNO (0.202 m3•g−1). The results suggest that after decoration of CQDs, the basic structure of CNO has not changed, but the porosity and specific surface area are increased. It may be originated from the evaporation of CQDs aqueous to create pores during the in-situ thermal polymerization process. Combined with improved visible-light absorption, optimized porous structure is conducive to the increase of the heterocatalytic interfaces, therefore enhance the feasibility to improved catalytic activity.

XPS were characterized to investigate the surface chemical composition. Fig. 7 a gives the detailed XPS signals of CNO and CNOC-1.0 and the survey curves depict the presence of C, N and O with no other impurities. Fig. 7b-d display high resolution XPS curves of C, N and O element of CNOC-1.0. C 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6, 286.3 and 288.0 eV, which are interpreted as graphitic C, C-O bond and sp2-hybridized carbon as the N-C=N backbone [32]. There are also three peaks in the fitted curves of N 1s spectrum centered at 398.5, 399.5 and 400.9 eV. They present sp2-hybridized nitrogen of C-N=C, typical tertiary N-(C)3 groups and amino groups C-N-H, respectively [33]. In the high-resolution of O 1s spectrum, the peak observed at a binding energy of 531.5 eV is associated with C=O containing groups. The other peak observed at a binding energy of 532.6 eV reveals the presence of N-C-O/C-O bonds, which are attributed to the introduction of O elements [34].

The photocatalytic H2 production of various materials was evaluated with triethanolamine as a sacrificial reagent and in-situ deposited Pt as a co-catalyst under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm) in a conventional closed-circulation system. As shown in Fig. 8 a, after 1 h irradiation, 154.5 μmol H2 was detected on CNO. Under the same experimental conditions, obvious increasing of H2 production (316.6 μmol) could be observed on CNOC samples, which is nearly 2.05 times of that on CNO. The doping content of CQDs seriously affect the H2 production. First, the H2 evolution increased with the increasing of CQDs, and CNOC-1.0 shows the optimized activity (316.6 μmol), which is 2.1 times of CNO nanosheets. As the CQDs content further increased, the photocatalytic activity noticeably decreased. It might be attribute to the competition of photons absorption by CNO and excess CQDs, thus limiting the generation of photo-induced carriers [35].

To considering the photocatalyst stability and reusability, H2 production catalyzed by CNOC-1.0 cycledfor five times was also evaluated. The used photocatalyst was collected, washed and dried for the next recycling after each cycling photocatalytic experiment. It could be seen in Fig. 8b that after 5 intermittent applications, CNOC-1.0 exhibits a well photocatalytic activity stability. After 10 hours of reaction, the H2 production activity remains around 87%. The slightly decrease could be on account of the in evitable loss of mass. The high-stability of the CNOC photocatalyst maintained indicates the strong interactions between CQDs and CNO different from the single surface deposit.

So far, co-catalyst is still an indispensable condition for improving the photocatalytic efficiency. Noble metal co-catalyst is usually the first choice because of its lower Fermi level than most semiconductors. The formed Schottky barrier when the co-catalyst is deposited on the semiconductor surface can effectively prevent electron-hole recombination and extend the life of photo-generated carriers. In addition, co-catalyst deposited on the surface of the photocatalyst can be used as active sites and can also reduce the over-potential of the reduction reaction, which is conductive to the water splitting to H2 [36,37].

To verify the co-catalytic effect of Pt on CNO and CNOC samples, photocatalytic H2 production with different deposited Pt content (3 wt%, 2 wt%, 1 wt%, 0.5wt%, 0 wt%) were tested under the same experimental steps and conditions within 1 h irradiation. It can be clearly seen in Fig. 9 a that only trace amount of H2 could be detected without Pt, so co-catalysts are necessary in metal-free photocatalytic process. When the deposited Pt content increase from 0.5 wt% to 3 wt%, both CNO and CNOC-1.0 perform increased H2 production, and gradually tend to stabilized. It can be clearly found from the promotion trend line that Pt deposited on CNOC-1.0 sample shows a more significant co-catalytic effect. The reason for this result may be the optimized surface area of CNOC, which is conductive for Pt particle deposition, as well as the enhanced charge transport and transfer properties.

Improving visible light absorption is one of the important functions of CQDs modification of semiconductors. The UV-Vis absorption spectra above proves that the modified CNOC-1.0 shows obvious enhanced visible-light absorption. Fig. 9b reveals the relationship between the wavelength of monochromatic light and H2 evolution over CNO and CNOC-1.0 samples. With the increase of light wavelength, the H2 production gradually decrease, nearly in line with the light absorption. However, when the irradiated monochromatic light increase from 520 nm to 600 nm, contrary increased H2 could be tested on CNOC-1.0. It further confirms that modification by appropriate amount of CQDs could expand the photocatalytic performance of materials.

In addition of enlarged surface area and enhanced visible light absorption, improving the photo-generated charge transport is significant for semiconductor modification using CQDs with special conductivity. Therefore, the structure induced photoelectric properties of the samples were investigated by PL analysis and electrochemical experiments, which could reflect the generation, separation and transport of charge carriers intuitively.

According to the mechanisms of PL, the band-band PL spectrum can indirectly reflect the separation of photoinduced carriers. First, steady-state PL spectra of CNO and serious CNOC samples were carried out and shown in Fig. 10 a. For CNO nanosheet, a strong PL emission peak centered at about 460 nm is observed. After CQDs were loaded into CNO nanosheets, the PL intensity of CNOC was significantly quenched as the mass of CQDs increases, revealing that coupling with

CQDs plays remarkable role in suppressing the recombination of the photogenerated electron-holes, and the free electrons are conducive to participate in interfacial photocatalytic reactions [38]. It is revealed that the lowest PL intensity of CNOC-1.0 suggests its least charge recombination. PL lifetime can more directly reflect the effective carriers transmission. Fig. 10b shows the time-resolved PL decay curves of CNO and CNOC-1.0 excited by 330 nm laser at room temperature. The calculated photo-generated charge carriers’ lifetime were calculated by biexponential function fitting. The average lifetimes (τ) of CNO and CNOC-1.0 are 3.056 and 3.579 ns, respectively. The extended lifetime of photo-induced carriers over CNOC-1.0 further illustrates that the introduction of CQDs efficiently retard the recombination of carriers and enhance the possibility of charge carriers participating in the following photocatalytic reaction [39].

Photoelectrochemical response was utilized to further investigate the charge carriers’ separation and transmission efficiency. Fig. 10c shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy map (EIS) of CNO and CNOC-1.0 tested in dark. It appears clearly that the arc radius of semicircular Nyquist plot of CNOC-1.0 is much smaller than that of CNO. This result represents the lower magnitude of resistance force when the electron-hole pairs move in CNOC-1.0. In addition, transient photocurrent response (Fig. 10d) was other valid technique that can analyze the charge generation and transfer in the semiconductor materials. Under visible light irradiation, CNOC-1.0 shows a larger instantaneous photocurrent density than CNO (almost 1.8 folds), manifesting the better visible-light responsiveness of CNOC-1.0. The increased photocurrent values indicates the more photogenerated charge generation. After several "on-off" irradiation cycle experiments, the photocurrent responses of CNOC-1.0 almost maintain stable reversibly and reproducibly [40]. This result is consistent with the tendency of impendence response, clearly demonstrates that the incorporation of CQDs into CNO nanosheets effectively facilitate the generation of photo-induced charges and provide more chance to migrate to the surface of the catalysts for catalytic reaction [41].

To further analyze the catalytic reaction process, Mott-Schottky plots were carried under a saturated calomel electrode system to determine the band edges of the catalysts. Shown Fig. 11 a, the conduction band (CB) potential of CNO and CNOC-1.0 can be verified at -1.15 and -1.04 (V vs SCE, pH=7). The downshift of CB reduces the driving force for reduction thermodynamically, nevertheless the CB position was still much higher than the reduction potential of H+/H2 (-0.17 V vs SCE, pH=7) [42]. According to the formula ECB = EVB-Eg, combined with the estimated optical band gaps, the valance band (VB) position of CNO and CNOC-1.0 are calculated as +1.67 and +1.75 V (V vs SCE, pH=7).

Based on previous reported literatures and the abovementioned analysis, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was speculated (Fig. 11b). Under visible light (> 400 nm) irradiation, the CQDs modified oxygen carbon nitride nanosheet CNOC was excited to generate photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The down-conversion photoluminescence properties of CQDs enabled it to convert absorbed light to lower wavelength with high energy photons, therefore further promoted the photo-induced carriers generation on CNOC [43]. Meanwhile, the photosensitive effect of CQDs could also donate photo-induced electrons to the CB of CNOC, leading to extended visible light response and enhancive electrons for H2 release. Optimized charge transfer assists free electrons migrating quickly, and then trapped by electron acceptor CQDs through the tight contact between CQDs and CNO, thereby accelerating H2 generation from water under visible light irradiation.

In summary, we have successfully embedded CQDs into CNO materials through an in-situ two-step thermal polymerization method. Structure characterization and H2 production evaluation showed that the obtained hybrid 0D/2D materials performed optimized photoelectric properties and H2-production activity, while maintaining the basic skeleton structure of CNO. With a rise in the amount of CQDs, the H2 evolution rate of the catalysts appears in a parabolic trend of the rising amount of decorated CQDs. Among them, CNOC-1.0 has the most excellent visible-light-induced photocatalytic H2 evolution performance (316.6 μmol•h−1), which was 2.1 times with respect to CNO (154.5 μmol•h−1). Also, it exhibited good stability for long term irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity could be attributed to the enlarged specific surface areas and porosity, increased visible light collection ability and improved migration and separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. This work provides a simple process for constructing metal-free hybrid polymer semiconductors for increasing photocatalytic H2 evolution performance.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molstruc.2020.xxxxxx.

Jie Ou-Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Kai Zhu: Data curation. Xue Li: Data curation, Validation. Yuxin Zhu: Data curation, Validation. Yanhua Song: Funding acquisition. Yanjuan Cui: Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20_3152) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190981).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A metal-free polymeric photocatalyst for hydrogen production from water under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. C. Wang, K. Maeda, A. Thomas, K. Takanabe, G. Xin, J. M. Carlsson, K. Domen, M. Antonietti, A metal-free polymeric photocatalyst for hydrogen production from water under visible light, Nat. Mater.8 (2009) 76-80.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymeric photocatalysts based on graphitic carbon nitride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. W. Cao, J. X. Low, J. G. Yu, M. Jaroniec, Polymeric photocatalysts based on graphitic carbon nitride, Adv. Mater.27 (2015) 2150-2176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A graphitic-C3N4 “seaweed” architecture for enhanced hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Han, B. Wang, Y. Zhao, C. Hu, L. Qu, A graphitic-C3N4 “seaweed” architecture for enhanced hydrogen evolution, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.54 (2015) 11433-11437.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of contact interface between TiO2 and g-C3N4 on the photoreactivity of g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst: (001) vs (101) facets of TiO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. A. Huang, Q. Sun, K. L. Lv, Z. H. Zhang, M. Li, B. Li, Effect of contact interface between TiO2 and g-C3N4 on the photoreactivity of g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst: (001) vs (101) facets of TiO2, Appl. Catal. B-Environ.164 (2015) 420-427.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of ZnIn2S4-In2O3 hierarchical tubular heterostructures for efficient CO2 photoreduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.B. Wang, B. Y. Guan, X. W. Lou, Construction of ZnIn2S4-In2O3 hierarchical tubular heterostructures for efficient CO2 photoreduction, J. Am. Chen. Soc.140 (2018) 5037-5040.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: mpg-C(3)N(4)-Catalyzed selective oxidation of alcohols using O(2) and visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Z. Su, S. C. Mathew, G. Lipner, X. Z. Fu, M. Antonietti, S. Blechert, X. C. Wang, mpg-C(3)N(4)-Catalyzed selective oxidation of alcohols using O(2) and visible light, J. Am. Chem. Soc.132 (2010) 16299.
